nodes.label: Great Research Question Criteria
nodes.rationale: A great research question must be: (1) Surprising to experts â€” claims known or easily predicted by experts have no research value; (2) Fruitful â€” opens downstream programs and questions, not dead-end micro-improvements; (3) Foreclosing alternative explanations â€” rigorous enough that the claim holds via ablations, baselines, and confound analysis; (4) Feasible â€” completable with available resources, skills, and time. These criteria exist because selection of the problem has disproportionate influence on total research impact.

nodes.label: Surprisingness Criterion
nodes.rationale: Research must surprise domain experts. Novelty to the researcher alone is insufficient â€” it requires rigorous literature review to verify the claim is not already known. Failure mode: reviewer demonstrates prior art exists.

nodes.label: Fruitfulness Criterion
nodes.rationale: Great research opens entire new programs downstream (e.g., backpropagation, scaling laws). Failure mode: making novel but inconsequential claims â€” answered by asking 'so what?' early. The importance must also be communicated clearly in the paper.

nodes.label: Rigor and Claim Scope Criterion
nodes.rationale: Claims must not exceed what evidence can support. Experimentation design must track the actual claim scope. Narrower claims are more technically correct but less impactful â€” walking this tightrope is a necessary scientific skill. Example: testing on GSM8K alone supports claims only about GSM8K, not 'reasoning in general'.

nodes.label: Feasibility Criterion
nodes.rationale: Research must be completable with available resources, knowledge, skills, and time. Ambition and feasibility are in tension: more ambitious projects require more evidence, more confound foreclosure, and more alternative explanations to address.

nodes.label: Research Phases (Exploration â†’ Sharpening â†’ Execution â†’ Writing)
nodes.rationale: Four sequential phases: (1) Exploration â€” time-boxed sprint to discover a potentially surprising claim; (2) Research Question Sharpening â€” put candidate claims through criteria for surprisingness, fruitfulness, rigor, feasibility; (3) Experiment Execution â€” after internal review and alignment; (4) Paper Writing â€” begins only when a strong cohesive story emerges. Novel experiments and new directions emerge during execution as part of the process.

nodes.label: Research Claim as Prior Intuition
nodes.rationale: Every research question carries an implicit or explicit prior belief before any experiment. Because infinite things can be measured empirically, experiment choice must be guided by intuitions about what is true. Analogized to Einstein's hunch about equivalence between acceleration and gravity leading to general relativity.

hyperedges.label: Four Criteria for Research Question Quality
